FT_IC1.evidence: Data drawn from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, NIH-funded, 21 U.S. recruitment sites, baseline ages 9-10, period 2-3 follow-ups (2018-2021).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective linear regression models estimating associations between cyberbullying victimisation (lifetime and past 12-month) at period 2 and manic symptom sum score at period 3 follow-up, N=9095.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Acta Paediatrica (2025); no indication of duplicate or preprint of an already-included paper.

FT_IC2.evidence: The exposure variable is cyberbullying victimisation alone (yes/no lifetime or past 12-month). Per binding clarification: cyberbullying alone fails IC2 unless paired with a separately-measured phone/SM variable that has its own coefficient. Screen time appears only as a covariate (adjusted for in models), not as an IV or DV with its own reported coefficient. No phone/SM-specific variable appears as IV or DV anywhere in the paper (main tables, Table 2, or otherwise). FT-EC2 applies.

notes: Primary IV is cyberbullying victimisation; screen time is a covariate only. Cyberbullying alone fails IC2 per binding clarification in §2.4.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study (N=10,054, Year 3, 2019-2021), 21 research sites across the U.S., baseline 2016-2018.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: ordinal logistic regression models assessing associations between six media parenting practice categories and R-rated movie and mature-rated video game consumption.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in BMC Pediatrics (2025); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: searching all results (Table 2, Appendices D-G) for a phone/SM-specific estimate paired with an individual-trait variable. The DVs are R-rated movie consumption and mature-rated video game use (content ratings, not phone/SM variables). The IVs are media parenting practices (parental monitoring, bedroom screen use, mealtime screen use, etc.), none of which are phone/SM-specific variables. No phone/SM-specific variable (time on phone, time on SM, named platform, texting, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient anywhere in the paper. Per binding clarification §2.4: mature/R-rated content alone fails IC2. Step 2 not applicable. FT-EC2 applies.

notes: DVs are R-rated movies and mature-rated video games (content rating, not SM/phone modality). IVs are parenting practice scores. Neither side qualifies as a phone/SM variable. Mature-rated content alone fails IC2 per binding clarification.

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the U.S. ABCD Study (N=9,519), four annual assessments from baseline (2016-2018, ages 9-10) to Year 3 (2019-2021), 21 study sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: compositional data analysis (CoDA) with isometric log-ratio transformations and mixed-effects modeling to examine longitudinal changes in screen time (including digital socializing), sleep, and sports/exercise activity over four annual assessments.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in BMC Pediatrics (2026); no indication of duplicate.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: the paper reports numeric estimates for screen time subtypes over time, including digital socializing (texting + video chatting + social networking), with bootstrapped mean hours/day and 95% CIs at each timepoint, and change-from-baseline estimates (e.g., digital socializing increased 0.98 h/day from baseline to Year 3, 95% CI 0.51-1.45, p<0.001; Table 2, Table S2). However, the 'other side' of every analysis is time/assessment wave (a temporal/contextual variable) — mixed-effects models with time as the independent variable, not any individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, etc.). The paper is purely descriptive of longitudinal trends; no analysis pairs a phone/SM variable with an individual-trait-level variable on the other side. The only stratification is by sex (a demographic variable), which also does not qualify. Per §2.4: the other side must be individual-trait-level; demographic-only or contextual-only on the other side fails IC2. FT-EC2 applies.

notes: Digital socializing (texting + video chatting + social networking) is a qualifying phone/SM composite per Step 1, but the other side of every analysis is time/wave (contextual) or sex (demographic). No individual-trait-level variable appears on the other side anywhere in the paper, including supplementary tables S1-S2.
